### ASSESSMENT OR CO-OPERATIVE LIFE INSURANCE.

**§ 660, KY. STATS. Organization of company.**

Any number of persons desiring to form an organization for the purpose of transacting the business of life or casualty insurance, or both life and casualty insurance upon the co-operative or assessment plan, may associate together and effect such organization as prescribed in subdivision I of this article and not otherwise. *Acts 1891-93, ch. 171, §123.* Same as *Act of 1892*.

# PART III—DECISIONS.

*Life Insurance Decisions.*

**Action on Policy—Question for Jury.** Where insured was found dead with a pistol near him the question as to whether he had mind enough to know that if he fired the ball through his head it would be likely to cause death, and fired with that intention, was a question of fact for the jury.

Cited: 91 Ky. 212; 112 Ky. 461.

## Case 39. Wright's Admr. v. Northwestern Ins. Co., 11 R. 519 (1889). Supr. Ct.

*Barbour, J., affirming.*

**Application for Policy—Occupation of Insured—Mistake—Fraud.** When an insurance policy provided that it should be void if the insured, without the company's consent, should be engaged as a brakeman, parol evidence is inadmissible to prove that when the agent wrote the application he knew the insured was employed in a railroad yard and engaged in coupling cars, but nevertheless wrote it down that insured was a laborer, saying "That will cover it all". In this case there was no allegation of fraud or mistake.

Overruled by same case, 91 Ky. 208. (Case 51 herein.)

## Case 40. Hartford Life & Annuity Ins. Co. v. Hayden's Admr., 90 Ky. 39; 11 R. 993; 13 S. W. 585 (1890).

*Holt, J., affirming.*

**Action on Policy—Evidence.** Statements by an agent relative to insuring a person, but made after the insurance has been effected, are not competent evidence against the company.

**Authority of Local Agent—Waiver.** Although a policy provides that agents have no authority to receive any other money than the admission fees, yet where the general manager of the company openly and publicly held the agent out as having such authority, and the agent accordingly collected the renewal fees, the company can not claim a forfeiture because the money was not paid to it direct.

# PART III—DECISIONS.

*Life Insurance Decisions.*

**Case 264. Lockett v. Lockett, 26 R. 300; 80 S. W. 1152 (1904).**
*O'Rear, J., reversing.*

**Beneficiaries — Right to change.** Under Ky. Stats., Sec. 670, authorizing an insured to change the beneficiary in his policy, neither the original beneficiary nor the insurer can prevent such change if the person sought to be substituted is one of the class permitted by insurer's charter and the terms of the policy to become a beneficiary.

**Parol assignment.** An insured may, by parol, assign a life insurance policy to one having an insurable interest in his life.

**Beneficiaries — Facts showing intention to change.** Decedent, prior to his marriage to plaintiff, had a policy in which defendant, his father, was named as beneficiary. After decedent's marriage, his father suggested that the beneficiary should be changed to plaintiff, and shortly thereafter declared to his wife, and in the presence of others, that the insurance was for her benefit, and that he had given it to her for her protection. He delivered the policy to her, and she retained it until after his death. During his last sickness, which lasted only nine days, he attempted to write to the insurer to have the transfer made, but was too weak to do so, and died before the transfer was formally completed. **Held:** That such facts were sufficient to constitute a change of beneficiary entitling plaintiff to the proceeds of the policy.

**Case 265. Supreme Council Catholic Knights of Equity of the World v. Heineman, 25 R. 1604; 78 S. W. 406 (1904).**
*Barker, J., affirming.*

**Suicide — Insanity.** Evidence held sufficient to sustain a finding that insured who took his own life, did not have sufficient mind to understand that he was taking his own life by swallowing carbolic acid, so that there could be recovery on his life policy, notwithstanding a provision that it should not cover death by suicide, whether sane or insane.

PART III—DECISIONS.

*Fire Insurance Decisions.*

**Case 504.** Rogers v. Farmers' Mutual Aid Assn., 106 Ky. 371; 20 R. 1925; 50 S. W. 543 (1899).

*Burnam, J., reversing.*

**Forfeiture—Waiver—Mutual company—Agents—Soliciting agents.** The appellee was a mutual insurance company, covering a very limited territory, namely, the county of Mason. There were no dividends, no assessments were to be made, except to meet losses, the basis of the company was said to be self-preservation, and its object, to insure the farm property, barns, etc., of its members. The active officers of the company were solicitors, who, from the limited business and territory of the company, were largely familiar with the property insured by the company and its affairs. A defense was set up to a suit on a policy that the assured had taken out additional insurance without notifying the company. Appellant had taken out additional insurance in order to borrow money on mortgage on the property and notified Rowe, a soliciting agent, of the fact. These facts were set up in the petition, as well as the additional fact that after such notification, the insured had been assessed to bear his part of losses suffered by other members. The petition also showed that all the officers of the company consisted of a president, secretary, and four solicitors, one of whom was Rowe, who was authorized to solicit insurance, make contracts therefor, and sign and deliver policies. **Held:** That Rowe was such an agent and was charged with such duties as to make notice to him notice to the company. Moreover, the petition goes further and avers that the company itself had notice of the additional insurance. Thus a good cause of action is stated.

Cited: 106 Ky. 818.

**Case 505.** Bluegrass Ins. Co. v. Cobb, 109 Ky. 339; 22 R. 857; 58 S. W. 981 (1900).

*DuRelle, J., reversing.*

**Attaching of liability—Kentucky Statutes.** One Cobb brought suit against appellant company for a loss by fire, alleging that he applied for insurance upon his dwelling house

Both the interest of the company and protection of the public require this to be the rule; and, as held in Marcus v. St. Louis Mutual Life Ins. Co., 68 N. Y., 625, a clause in a policy that agents are not authorized to vary, alter or discharge contracts should not be regarded as applying to general agents.

Cited: 107 Ky. 249.

## Case 41. Hexter v. United States Life Ins. Co., 11 R. 903 (1890). Supr. Ct.

*Bowden, J., affirming.*

**Paid-Up Policy—Default in Payments—Option.** The Kentucky rule is that where a policy of life insurance provides that in the event it shall cease by the non-payment of premiums the company will, upon its surrender within a certain time, issue a paid-up policy for a certain proportion of the original amount insured, the beneficiary can either return the policy and obtain a paid-up policy for the proper amount, or can retain the old policy and recover that amount upon it after the death of the insured. But even if the only right is to have a paid-up policy, that right is not lost by the failure to surrender the old policy within the time prescribed. The view of the New York and Pennsylvania courts, however, is different, and there the only liability on the company after default is to issue a paid-up policy, and there can be no recovery on the old policy for any amount. And whether or not, under that rule, the right to demand a paid-up policy is lost by a failure to apply within the prescribed time, it may be barred by the statute of limitations.

**Limitation of Actions on Policies—Conflict of Laws.** As the defendant insurance company in this case is a New York corporation, and the insured and his wife, the beneficiary in the policy sued on, were citizens of, and residents in, Pennsylvania when the policy was issued, the policy is either a New York or a Pennsylvania contract, and the construction placed upon such contracts by the courts of those States must be regarded as a part of the policy,

*Life Insurance Decisions.*

**Case 265a. American Benevolent Assn. v. Stough, 26 R. 1093; 83 S. W. 126 (1904).**
*Hobson, J., affirming.*

**Suicide — Presumption against — Question for jury.** When the dead body of insured is found under such circumstances as to indicate that his death may have resulted from negligence, accident or suicide, the presumption of the law is against suicide, as contrary to the general conduct of mankind, and where the evidence is circumstantial only, and admits of more than one reasonable conclusion, the question is for the jury.

**Suicide—Evidence warranting finding against.** Where insured, a railroad man, with pleasant domestic relations, after talking pleasantly in an ordinary manner with friends in a barroom, and being sober, left the bar and went to the back yard, where there was a water closet, and just after he reached the yard a pistol shot was heard, and he was found on the brick walk with a bullet wound in his head and with a pistol lying near his feet, and there was no assignable cause for his killing himself, a verdict for plaintiff was held not against the weight of evidence.

**Action on policy—Instruction as to intentional injury.** Where a policy provided that it should not extend to or cover death resulting from suicide or intentional injury, an instruction to find for plaintiff, unless insured died from the effects of a gunshot wound intentionally inflicted by himself or some other person, was proper.

**Action on policy—Evidence as to suicide.** When the defense in an action on a policy was suicide, and the burden was assumed by defendant, it was proper for plaintiff in cross-examining a witness who testified to the manner of insured's death, to bring out evidence that the pistol with which insured was shot might have dropped at his feet and shot him, or that some one might have killed him, and that the witness did not know how insured came to be shot, this evidence being proper to show the jury the limits of the witness' knowledge.

**Action on policy.** The proofs of loss, consisting of affidavits of persons other than the beneficiary, were not competent as substantive evidence for or against the bene-

All this was stricken out. Held: That Sec. 702, Ky. Stats., does not make one a member of an assessment insurance company merely upon making application, but the person so signing such application must also be insured in such a corporation, and while the language of the section is awkward in disregarding the order of the two events in point of time, it seems undoubtedly to require two things to concur in order to make such person a member of the company—namely, that he shall sign the application for insurance and shall become insured. This case was submitted to the court upon the law and the facts, but the findings of fact do not support the judgment.

*Life Insurance Decisions.*

"If after two or more annual premiums shall have been paid in cash, default shall be made in payment of any premium or interest on the day when it shall become due", the company "will issue a non-participating policy, . . . provided this policy be then freed from all indebtedness to the company, and provided also that written application be made therefor, and this policy and all interest therein be surrendered in the lifetime of the insured, and within six months from the date of such default". Held: That the insured was entitled to have issued a paid-up, non-participating policy, notwithstanding he may not have surrendered his policy, freed of indebtedness, within six months from default. A court of equity will not enforce these provisions which are in the nature of forfeitures. And this is true, although the company be a mutual company and although it may have distributed its profits and surplus money among its members each year as authorized by its charter.

**Case 47.** Northwestern Mutual Life Ins. Co. v. Barbour, 92 Ky. 427; 13 R. 619; 17 S. W. 796; 85 L. R. A. 49 (1891).
*Holt, C. J., reversing.*

**Forfeiture—Penalties—Paid-Up Insurance.** A policy of life insurance stipulated that it should cease if the premiums be not paid when due, but gave the insured the right to a paid-up policy, provided all his indebtedness to the company shall be paid and the policies surrendered within six months from the time of default. It was held that the time within which assured was to surrender his policy and settle his indebtedness was of the essence of the contract; and that such surrender and settlement was a condition precedent to the right to obtain a paid-up policy and where such surrender and settlement was not made within the time limited, the company was discharged.

It is not like the case where the party has received the entire consideration for performance on his part and the other party merely fails to demand performance within a stipulated time; but by the terms of the contract it has by the default of the party wholly determined, but the privilege is given to him, provided he does certain things

**Case 270.** Kentucky & Louisville Mutual Ins. Co. v. Southard, 8 B. Mon. 634 (1848).
*Marshall, C. J., affirming.*

**Representations — Warranties.** The general rule is well settled that an express warranty must appear on the face of the policy, and that instructions for insurance, unless inserted in the policy itself, do not amount to a warranty.

**Survey—Descriptions therein are representations.** Where a policy refers to a survey as forming part of the policy, the descriptions contained in the survey are to be considered as representations and not warranties.

**Material representation.** In order that a policy shall be avoided for a false representation, it is necessary that the representation be material and that the insurer was in fact misled thereby. Its materiality should be pleaded.

**Representation—Description—Dwelling house.** The representation of a house as a dwelling house is not falsified by averment and proof that one of its rooms was used as a kitchen. And a statement in the survey that there was a kitchen and negro building in the rear is not entirely equivalent to a representation that there was no kitchen in the dwelling house. (See Pleading—Fire Insurance).

**Action on policy — Issues and evidence.** Oral representations of insured to the surveyor, made before issual of policy, are not admissible under a plea that the building was used for a more hazardous purpose than represented.

**Avoidance by increased hazard.** To avoid a policy for use for a more hazardous purpose, it must be shown that insurance could not have been made at that rate or at all but for such misrepresentations.

**Pleading—Representation.** A plea attempting to avoid a policy on the ground of misrepresentation should show clearly that the fact misrepresented was material; and it is not sufficient merely to show that the actual hazard of loss was greater as the fact existed than it may have been supposed to be as the fact was represented.

# INTRODUCTION.

This book contains, in Part I, all the statutes passed prior to 1893, with an index. These older laws have been superseded, but historically they are of importance, besides they are useful for comparison and there are decisions construing some of them which aid in the interpretation of similar statutes now in force; and old policies written prior to 1893 are governed by them.

Part II comprises all the insurance statutes now in force, with amendments, the original or repealed section also being given. These statutes are annotated by references to decisions and followed by a complete index. Chronological arrangement of the statutes has been adopted in both Part I and Part II, and adhered to except when it was necessary to place an amendment near the original law or for some other apparent special reason.

Part III contains digests of all the decisions of the Appellate Courts of Kentucky divided into life, fire and other kinds of insurance so that the cases relating to each subject are placed together and arranged chronologically in these several divisions. It is followed by an index covering completely all the cases.

The indexes to the statutes and the decisions have received particular attention and much effort has been expended upon them to make them full, plain, comprehensive and practically useful to lawyers, insurance companies, agents and others who may be interested in the subject of insurance.

## § 712, KY. STATS. Assessment—action to recover—exclusion for failure to pay.

An action may be brought by the corporation against any member thereof to recover all assessments which he may neglect or refuse to pay, made upon him under the provisions of this act, or the by-laws of the corporation. ## Fire Insurance Decisions.

**Taxation—Contingent liability to pay losses.** The contingent liability of an insurance company to pay losses, and to refund unearned premiums, is not an indebtedness which may be deducted from its assets when listing its property for taxation; and in the absence of a provision of its charter, or of a statute to the contrary, a fund required by statute to be preserved free from dividends, consisting of certain securities, interest and the amount received from premiums upon unexpired policies, declared to be unearned premiums, is liable to municipal taxation.

**Case 328.** London & Lan. Fire Ins. Co. v. Turnbull, 86 Ky. 230; 9 R. 544; 5 S. W. 542 (1887).
*Bennett, J., affirming.*

**Cancellation of policy—Notice of.** Where a policy of fire insurance provides that the company may, at its option, terminate the insurance at any time by giving notice and refunding the unearned portion of the premium, such notice means notice to the insured. And the company can not cancel the policy merely by giving notice to its own agents to cancel it. And when notice is not given the insured before the loss, the company is liable on the policy.

**Loss prorated on policies written with consent of assured.** Under a policy limiting recovery to proportion of loss as the sum insured bears to the total amount of valid or invalid insurance, the insured is bound by the contract if additional insurance, valid or invalid is effected with his authority and consent.

This does not apply to policies issued without the knowledge, consent or ratification of insured.

**The Contract—Mutuality.** A person who was the agent for two fire insurance companies, upon being notified by one of them to cancel a certain policy, proceeded to issue a policy in the other company to the assured in lieu of the first company's policy. But this policy the assured refused to accept, and it appeared that the company itself knew nothing about the transaction. It was held that the consent of the parties being wanting, there was no contract of insurance.

*Hobson, J., affirming.*

**Application as part of policy.** On October 5th, 1897, appellant issued to T. H. Puryear a policy of insurance on his life for $10,000. On March 18, 1898, Puryear died. Suit was brought on the policy. The company defended on the ground that Puryear had committed suicide and that it was stipulated in its application that the company was not to be liable if he committed suicide within two years after the policy was issued, whether "sane or insane, voluntary or involuntary". This condition was not contained in the policy; but by its terms the application was made a part of the contract. The application was not attached to the policy, and the court below held that under sections 656, 679 of the Kentucky Statutes, the statements in the application could not be relied on to defeat the action on the policy. *Held correct.* Under those statutes it is evident that the purpose of the Legislature was that the assured might know from his policy what his contract was, and that contracts not contained in the policy or written upon the back of it or attached to it should not be considered in order to avoid imposition by agents eager to earn commission, and to avoid the litigation incident thereto after the death of the assured when his tongue had been forever silenced and any explanation he might have made could not be told.

**Case 135.** New York Life Ins. Co. v. Weaver's Admr., 114 Ky. 295; 24 R. 1086; 70 S. W. 628 (1902).
Burnam, J., affirming.

**Fraud—Misrepresentations—Rescission—Reasonable time to sue—Incontestable clause.** Mary L. Weaver died insured in the appellant company for $10,000. After her death suit on the policy was brought by the beneficiaries, namely, her administrator as to one-half and her children as to the other half and the full amount, interest and costs paid into court and withdrawn by the plaintiffs and judgment of satisfaction entered. Afterwards the appellant insurance company brought this suit to recover from the beneficiaries the money paid, alleging that the policy had been obtained by fraudulent misrepresentations of the insured and of their own examining surgeon conspiring with the insured. The company does not allege want of knowledge of the fraud before the payment of the money.

Held: That rescission of a contract will be afforded to an innocent complaining party provided he has not acted upon it or recognized it as binding after the discovery of the fraud if the right of rescission is exercised within a reasonable time. And a court of chancery here, notwithstanding the incontestable clause, would have canceled the policy in a suit for rescission brought within a reasonable time after its issual and during the lifetime of the insured upon the tender back of the premiums received by the company. In this case the company failed to take any steps looking to a repudiation of the contract during the lifetime of the insured and after her death confessed liability by a payment of the principal, interest and costs.

**Action on policy—Res judicata.** Held: That in the suit brought by the beneficiaries the defendant could have litigated the matters set up here and that said suit decides not only every question which is presented, but every one which ought to have been presented or such as are essentially involved in the subject matter of the litigation especially as to matters of claim and demand. Having failed to institute a suit for rescission within a reasonable time or to make defense when sued upon the policy, appellant can not be permitted to relitigate questions which were necessarily concluded by the judgment.

Union Central Life Ins. Co., 187 U. S. 362, affirming 105 Fed. 419.

C. The insured became insane and took his life. The fact as to whether he was legally or morally responsible or could control his acts is not decisive, but it does not appear that he knew that what he was doing would end his life. Therefore, there can be a full recovery upon the policy notwithstanding that the recovery was limited in case he should die by his own hand and was insane. It is as though he had died by accident. ## Taxation Generally.

### § 4080, KY. STATS. Foreign corporations—value of franchise—how determined.

If the corporation, company or association be organized under the laws of any other State or government, except as provided in the next section, the board shall fix the capital stock as hereinbefore provided, and will determine from the amount of the gross receipts of such corporation, company or association in this State and elsewhere, the proportion which the gross receipts in this State, within twelve months next before the fifteenth day of September of the year in which the assessment was made, bears to the entire gross receipts of the company, the same proportion of the value of the entire capital stock, less the assessed value of the tangible property assessed, or liable to assessment, in this State, shall be the correct value of the corporate franchise of such corporation, company or association for taxation in this State.

### § 4091, KY. STATS. (1903). When taxes due—penalties.

All State taxes assessed against any corporation, company or association under this article, except banks and trust companies, shall be due and payable thirty days after notice of the same has been given said corporation, company or association by the Auditor; and all county, municipal, school and other taxes shall be due and payable thirty days after notice of the amount of said tax is given by the officer, whose duty it is to collect the same, and every such corporation, company or association failing to pay its taxes, after receiving thirty days' notice, shall be deemed delinquent, and a penalty of ten per cent on the amount of the tax shall attach, and thereafter such tax shall bear interest at the rate of ten per cent per annum; any such corporation, company or association failing to pay its taxes, penalty and interest, after becoming delinquent, shall be deemed guilty of a misdemeanor, and, on conviction, shall be fined fifty dollars for each day the same remains unpaid, to be recovered by indictment or civil action, of which the Franklin Circuit Court shall have jurisdiction. **Case 165. Scott's Admr. v. Scott, 25 R. 1356; 77 S. W. 1122 (1904).**
*Settle, J., affirming.*

**Insurable Interest—Wagering policy.** The beneficiary of a life insurance policy, which was obtained by decedent voluntarily for her benefit, had in good faith married him in the belief that he had been divorced from his first wife, and lived with him many years without ever being informed that there was even a doubt of the legality of her marriage until after his death, though his first wife and her daughters had every opportunity to advise her of it long before his death. Besides this, she and her children were absolutely dependent on decedent for support. Held that, independent of the validity of her marriage, she had an insurable interest in his life, and that the contract could not be regarded as within the rule against wagering policies.

**Right to proceeds—Divorce and remarriage.** In an action to determine the right to life insurance it was conclusively shown that deceased had separated from his first wife more than 15 years before his death; that he left the State avowedly to live in another State long enough to get a divorce; that following his return after a long absence he married a second wife, and after her death a third, in whose favor the policy was drawn; that he lived with them for more than 30 years, publicly recognizing each in turn; that the first wife, who continued to reside in the same place during that time, lived near enough to meet the second before her death and to see the third often, and was never known to deny that he had been divorced from her, or to make complaint of his marriage to either; and, in addition to that, she permitted two of her daughters to live some time with their father and his third wife. Held, That the presumption of the legality of the last marriage created by this showing shifted to the first wife and others claiming with her the burden of establishing its legality.

# PART III—DECISIONS.
*Life Insurance Decisions.*

**Life policy for family—Construction of.** A life policy for the benefit of the family of the person procuring it is in the nature of a testament and in considering it the courts should treat it as far as possible as a will, as in so doing they will more nearly approximate the intention of the persons, the designation of whose bounty is involved in such cases.

Cited: 92 Ky. 327.

## Case 172. McLin v. Calvert, 78 Ky. 472 (1880).
*Cofer, J., reversing.*

**Charter—Widow and children—Distribution—Proceeds of certificate.** Under the charter it was provided that the fund was for the benefit of the widow and children of the deceased member. Held: That the fund should be distributed as the surplus personalty under the statute, and not per capita.

Cited: 101 Ky. 274.

## Case 173. Throckmorton's Admr. v. National Mut. Ben. Assn., 4 R. 61 (1882). Louisville Chy. Ct., Edwards, Chancellor.

**Assignment of policy—Insurable interest—Public policy.** An assignment of insurance policy to one having no insurable interest in the life of the assured, is invalid, as a wagering contract prohibited by public policy. In general an insurable interest, which takes a contract out of the class of wager policies, may be stated to be such an interest as exists in a creditor or surety of the assured, or such as arises from the ties of blood or marriage, in such relations to the assured as will raise a reasonable presumption of an expectation of benefit from the continuance of his life, or of such natural affection as will tend to create a desire to protect his life.

**Assignment of policy to creditor—Validity.** Assignment of life policy by the assured to his creditor, is valid and effectual to the extent of the actual indebtedness of the assured to the creditor, but beyond that extent, is invalid, as a wagering contract prohibited by public policy.

*Life Insurance Decisions.*

**Case 174. Basye v. Adams, 81 Ky. 368; 5 R. 91 (1883).**
*Lewis, J., affirming.*

**Assignment of certificate.** The assignment to a person of a certificate of membership in a life insurance association does not confer upon him the rights and privileges of a member. It appeared that the charter of the company required the consent of the company to become a member and that members must possess certain qualifications.

**Assignment of certificate—Assignee not "beneficiary."** The assignee of a policy of life insurance is not a "beneficiary"; and where the charter of an insurance society provides that the fund shall be paid out to the "legal heirs or beneficiary" of the member, it is not in the power of the member by assignment to divest both the heirs and beneficiary of the fund.

**Assignment of certificate—Insurable interest.** "Public policy requires that in all cases it should be essential to the validity of a policy of insurance, obtained by one person for his own benefit upon the life of another, that the party so obtaining the policy should have an insurable interest in the life of that other.

"It is not easy to define with precision what will, in all cases, constitute an insurable interest, so as to take the contract out of the class of wager policies. It may be stated generally, however, to be such an interest arising from the relations of the party obtaining the insurance, either as creditor of or surety for the assured, or from the ties of blood or marriage to him, as will justify a reasonable expectation of advantage or benefit from the continuance of his life. It is not necessary that the expectation of advantage or benefit should always be capable of pecuniary estimation; for a parent has an insurable interest in the life of his child, and a child in the life of his parent, a husband in the life of his wife, and a wife in the life of her husband. In all cases, there must be a reasonable ground, founded upon the relations of the parties to each other, either pecuniary or of blood or affinity, to expect some benefit or advantage from the continuance of the life of the assured. Otherwise, the contract is a mere wager, by which the party taking the policy is deeply in-

*Fire Insurance Decisions.*

**Case 470.** Orient Ins. Co. v. Clark, 22 R. 1066; 59 S. W. 863 (1900).

Burnam, J., affirming. Wind Insurance.

**Proofs of loss — Forfeiture for failure to furnish not implied.** Where the policy does not provide that the failure to furnish proofs of loss shall operate as a forfeiture, the court will not imply a forfeiture on that account.

**Proof of loss — Waiver.** Though the policy provides that the assured can not maintain his action without making proofs of loss, yet if the company denies its liability for any sum, even after the expiration of the time prescribed for making such proofs, it waives its right thereto.

**Action on policy—Departure in pleading.** Where plaintiff sued on a policy alleged to be for a term of three years, and defendant by its answer alleged that the policy was for a term of only one year, a reply alleging that the policy, by mistake, read "one year" instead of "three years," did not state a new cause of action.

**Case 471.** Palatine Ins. Co. v. Weiss; Merchants' Ins. Co. v. Weiss, 109 Ky. 464; 22 R. 994; 59 S. W. 509 (1900).

Burnam, J., affirming.

**Valued policy law—Total loss.** "It is the opinion of the court that the words 'total loss,' when applied to a building, do not mean that the materials of which the building was composed were all totally destroyed and obliterated. It is not necessary that all the parts and materials composing the building should be absolutely and physically destroyed, but the inquiry always is, does the insured building after the fire still exist, preserving substantially its identity, or has it become so broken and disintegrated that it can not be designated as the structure which was insured. There may be a total loss within the meaning of the statute even though some parts of the building may remain standing after the fire." In this case the evidence as to the condition of the building is conflicting, and the facts were submitted to the jury under a proper construction of the statute as outlined above, and the jury found for the plaintiff and the judgment thereon will not be disturbed.

**Right to proceeds—Divorce and remarriage.** In an action to determine the right to life insurance it was conclusively shown that deceased had separated from his first wife more than 15 years before his death; that he left the State avowedly to live in another State long enough to get a divorce; that following his return after a long absence he married a second wife, and after her death a third, in whose favor the policy was drawn; that he lived with them for more than 30 years, publicly recognizing each in turn; that the first wife, who continued to reside in the same place during that time, lived near enough to meet the second before her death and to see the third often, and was never known to deny that he had been divorced from her, or to make complaint of his marriage to either; and, in addition to that, she permitted two of her daughters to live some time with their father and his third wife. Held, That the presumption of the legality of the last marriage created by this showing shifted to the first wife and others claiming with her the burden of establishing its legality.

**Insurable Interest—Wagering policy.** The beneficiary of a life insurance policy, which was obtained by decedent voluntarily for her benefit, had in good faith married him in the belief that he had been divorced from his first wife, and lived with him many years without ever being informed that there was even a doubt of the legality of her marriage until after his death, though his first wife and her daughters had every opportunity to advise her of it long before his death. Besides this, she and her children were absolutely dependent on decedent for support. Held that, independent of the validity of her marriage, she had an insurable interest in his life, and that the contract could not be regarded as within the rule against wagering policies.

## Case 173. Throckmorton's Admr. v. National Mut. Ben. Assn., 4 R. 61 (1882). Louisville Chy. Ct., Edwards, Chancellor.

**Assignment of policy—Insurable interest—Public policy.** An assignment of insurance policy to one having no insurable interest in the life of the assured, is invalid, as a wagering contract prohibited by public policy. In general an insurable interest, which takes a contract out of the class of wager policies, may be stated to be such an interest as exists in a creditor or surety of the assured, or such as arises from the ties of blood or marriage, in such relations to the assured as will raise a reasonable presumption of an expectation of benefit from the continuance of his life, or of such natural affection as will tend to create a desire to protect his life.

**Assignment of policy to creditor—Validity.** Assignment of life policy by the assured to his creditor, is valid and effectual to the extent of the actual indebtedness of the assured to the creditor, but beyond that extent, is invalid, as a wagering contract prohibited by public policy.

**Assignment of certificate—Insurable interest.** "Public policy requires that in all cases it should be essential to the validity of a policy of insurance, obtained by one person for his own benefit upon the life of another, that the party so obtaining the policy should have an insurable interest in the life of that other.

"It is not easy to define with precision what will, in all cases, constitute an insurable interest, so as to take the contract out of the class of wager policies. It may be stated generally, however, to be such an interest arising from the relations of the party obtaining the insurance, either as creditor of or surety for the assured, or from the ties of blood or marriage to him, as will justify a reasonable expectation of advantage or benefit from the continuance of his life. It is not necessary that the expectation of advantage or benefit should always be capable of pecuniary estimation; for a parent has an insurable interest in the life of his child, and a child in the life of his parent, a husband in the life of his wife, and a wife in the life of her husband. In all cases, there must be a reasonable ground, founded upon the relations of the parties to each other, either pecuniary or of blood or affinity, to expect some benefit or advantage from the continuance of the life of the assured. Otherwise, the contract is a mere wager, by which the party taking the policy is deeply in-

**Beneficiaries — Equities in favor of divorced wife with infant children.** In this case it is the manifest purpose that the Widows' and Orphans' Fund, created by the charter of the Knights of Honor, should go to the widow and infant children who are dependent.

A policy was taken out by Peter Leaf for the benefit of his wife by his second marriage. Before he died, this wife, Lucy, obtained a divorce, and the estate, which had doubtless been created by both of them was divided between them, the certificate being retained by her, and she ever thereafter keeping up the dues thereon until he died. Before his death he sought to change the beneficiary of the certificate, making it payable to an adult son and his brothers and sisters, children by the first wife of the said Peter. The divorced wife refused to surrender the certificate, and had no notice that he had appointed another beneficiary.

Held: That the divorced wife had such equities as entitled her to the fund in preference to the adult children by the first marriage.

*Brent, J., affirming.*

**Beneficiaries—Laws of order.** A certificate in the Knights of Honor, a mutual benefit association, was payable to the wife and children of the insured member. The wife died. The husband married again and died, leaving his second wife surviving. In a contest between said widow and the children of the first marriage, it is held that said children were entitled to the fund by reason of the general laws of the association, which provided that "In event of the death of one or more of the persons selected by the member before the death of such member, if he shall make no further disposition thereof, upon his death such benefit shall be paid in full to the surviving beneficiary or beneficiaries."

**Case 231. Caudell v. Woodward, 96 Ky. 646; 16 R. 742; 29 S. W. 614 (1895).**

*Guffy, J., reversing.*

**Beneficiaries—Charter provisions—Wills—Legacy.** The contest here is over $1,921.00, payable under a certificate of membership in the Kentucky Grangers' Mutual Benefit Society. This sum is claimed by the wife and also by the children of the member. The will of the member devised to his wife all his estate, including life insurance, providing out of the same the wife should pay the debts and also use the same for the children's benefit as she might think proper. In case of her re-marriage she was only to have a child's share. The charter of the association provides that the benefit fund is for the wife and children, but that the deceased might otherwise dispose of it.

Held: That the true construction of the will is that the appellant wife was to have the entire sum of money and that the lower court erred in giving judgment against her in any amount, she having collected the sum.

**Fire insurance — Parol contract.** A parol contract of insurance made with an insurance agent representing two companies—the company to take the risk not being specified—is not enforceable.

**Agents — Power to bind company by parol contract.** Where an insurance agent had previously attempted to secure insurance for an applicant on a house tenanted by negroes, and failed to induce his company to take such risk, it is notice to the applicant of the limitation of the agent's authority in that regard, so that a subsequent parol contract of insurance made with him, and covering other property so inhabited, does not bind the company.
